symptoms.symptom_name: Autonomic prodrome (visual 'greying out' and lightheadedness) before loss of consciousness

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A gradual autonomic prodrome (visual greying, lightheadedness) is characteristic of neurally mediated reflex syncope and is one of the strongest discriminators from cardiac syncope.
Cardiac syncope is typically sudden and unheralded; a clear prodrome argues against it.
An autonomic (rather than epileptic aura) prodrome and the subsequent rapid recovery favour syncope over seizure.

symptoms.symptom_name: Trigger: prolonged standing in a warm environment (with mild dehydration)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Prolonged upright standing in a warm room promotes venous pooling and triggers the vasovagal reflex - a classic situational precipitant.
Orthostatic hypotension characteristically causes symptoms immediately on rising from sitting/lying, not after prolonged sustained standing.

symptoms.symptom_name: Rapid, complete, spontaneous recovery within ~30 seconds with no postictal confusion

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Rapid full recovery once supine, without prolonged confusion, is typical of reflex syncope as cerebral perfusion is quickly restored.
A prolonged postictal confusional state is expected after a generalised seizure; its absence with immediate orientation argues strongly against epilepsy.

symptoms.symptom_name: Bystander-observed marked pallor during the episode

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Profound pallor reflects the reflex vasodilatation/bradycardia and hypoperfusion of a vasovagal faint.
Marked pallor is more typical of syncope than of a seizure (which is more often associated with cyanosis/flushing).

symptoms.symptom_name: Witnessed occipital head strike with a tender scalp haematoma

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Traumatic intracranial injury from the fall
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A witnessed head strike with a scalp haematoma is the mechanism of concern for a delayed intracranial bleed and mandates same-day clinical clearance and safety-netting, independent of the benign faint.

symptoms.symptom_name: No exertional or no-warning onset, no palpitations, no chest pain

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Exertional/unheralded onset, palpitations and chest pain are the cardinal features of dangerous cardiac syncope; their absence substantially lowers its probability.
A warned, non-exertional faint without palpitations is consistent with a benign reflex mechanism.

symptoms.symptom_name: No tongue-biting and no urinary/faecal incontinence

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Lateral tongue-biting and incontinence favour a generalised seizure; their absence supports syncope over epilepsy.

symptoms.symptom_name: No family history of sudden or unexplained death at a young age

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of a family history of premature sudden death lowers the pre-test probability of an inheritable arrhythmia/cardiomyopathy syndrome as the cause of a first syncope.

symptoms.symptom_name: Normal neurological examination, negative active-stand orthostatic vitals, and no cardiac murmur (clinician-measured)

investigation_details.investigation_name: In-person neurological and cardiovascular examination with active-stand orthostatic vitals
investigation_details.expected_result: Cranial nerves II-XII intact, normal power/sensation/coordination, no nystagmus, equal reactive pupils; regular rate and rhythm with dual heart sounds and no murmur; no significant postural BP drop on active-stand testing; a small non-fluctuant occipital haematoma with no bony step-off or laceration
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Without in-person examination, a focal neurological deficit, a significant orthostatic drop, a murmur of structural heart disease, or a concerning head injury cannot be excluded.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A normal neurological and cardiac examination with negative orthostatic vitals supports a benign reflex faint and helps clinically clear the head injury.

symptoms.symptom_name: Baseline resting 12-lead ECG (to exclude an occult arrhythmic substrate)

investigation_details.expected_result: Expected to be normal in benign vasovagal syncope; screens for occult substrates (prolonged QT, pre-excitation/WPW, Brugada pattern, conduction disease) that would reclassify the syncope as potentially arrhythmic
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: An occult arrhythmic substrate presenting as a first syncope could be missed if no baseline ECG is obtained.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A baseline 12-lead ECG is the standard low-yield screen recommended for any first syncope to exclude an occult arrhythmic cause; a normal ECG further supports the benign diagnosis.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: The combination of an autonomic prodrome, a prolonged-standing warm-environment trigger, bystander pallor, and rapid complete recovery without postictal confusion is a high-specificity constellation for benign reflex syncope, especially when the dangerous-syncope screen (exertional/no-warning onset, palpitations, chest pain, family history of sudden death) is negative.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person neurological and cardiovascular examination with active-stand orthostatic vitals (same day)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Clears the head injury, excludes a focal neurological deficit and a murmur of structural heart disease, and quantifies any postural BP drop.
Baseline screen for an occult arrhythmic substrate (long QT, pre-excitation, Brugada) recommended for any first syncope; expected normal here.

Only if head-injury red flags develop or clinical assessment raises concern (e.g. focal deficit, worsening headache, vomiting, drowsiness, anticoagulation) - not routinely required for a minor strike with a normal neurological examination.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Emergency ambulance for a fully recovered, red-flag-negative reflex faint
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Unnecessary over-triage for the syncope itself in a recovered young adult with no red flags; prompt same-day in-person review is appropriate, reserving an ambulance for evolving head-injury or dangerous-syncope red flags.
